title: Suspected Fracture Red Flags for Physio
synonyms: Suspected Fracture Red Flags for Physio

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Physiotherapists in remote or occupational settings need a low threshold to suspect fracture when pain, mechanism, and function do not fit a simple soft tissue injury.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Focal bony tenderness
- Inability to weight-bear or use the limb normally
- Significant swelling, deformity, or bruising
- Pain out of proportion to apparent soft tissue findings
- Pain after fall, twist, crush, direct blow, or repetitive bone-loading history

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider occult fracture in older patients, stress fractures in load-related presentations, and pathological fractures when pain is disproportionate or atraumatic
- Compartment syndrome is a time-critical surgical emergency - suspect it when pain is out of proportion to the injury, pain worsens on passive stretch of muscles in the compartment, or there is paraesthesia or progressive weakness in the limb. Presence of a pulse does not exclude compartment syndrome. Escalate immediately
- Neurovascular compromise, open injury, or compartment concerns are immediate escalation triggers
- Do not repeatedly stress-test a limb when fracture suspicion is meaningful

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen mechanism, bony tenderness, deformity, and function
- Stop provocative testing if fracture seems possible
- Protect the area, reduce load, and escalate for medical review or imaging

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Use regional rules such as Ottawa ankle guidance only where validated and appropriate
- Distinguish likely stress injury from acute fracture based on onset, load history, and focal tenderness
- Liaise for imaging or urgent transfer when fracture, dislocation, or neurovascular compromise is suspected

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - A patient who can still move a limb may still have a fracture
